My Shanty, Lake George
Barbara Buhler Lynes
Early works of Georgia O'Keeffe
Georgia O'Keeffe

O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914
Red and Orange Streak
sentences: The Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio is a historic house museum at 12 Palvadera Drive in Abiquiú, New Mexico.
It was from 1945 until 1984 the principal residence and studio of artist Georgia O'Keeffe (1887-1986).
It is now owned and managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, through which guided tours can be arranged.
It was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998, as one of the most important artistic sites in the southwestern United States.
My Shanty, Lake George is a 1922 painting by Georgia O'Keeffe.
From 1918 to 1934, Georgia O'Keeffe spent part of the year at Alfred Stieglitz's family estate in Lake George.

The depicted shanty was O'Keeffe's studio, which was painted in subdued tones in response to criticism from Stieglitz' circle—Arthur Dove, John Marin, Charles Demuth, Marsden Hartley, and Paul Strand.

O'Keeffe said of the painting: "The clean, clear colors were in my head, but one day as I looked at the brown burned wood of the Shanty I thought, "I can paint one of those dismal-colored paintings like the men.
I think just for fun I will try—all low-toned and dreary with the tree beside the door."
"My Shanty" was the first painting by O'Keeffe purchased by the Duncan Phillips.
Barbara Buhler Lynes is an art historian, curator, professor, and preeminent scholar on the art and life of Georgia O'Keeffe.
She is currently the Sunny Kaufman Senior Curator at the NSU Museum of Art in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.

From 1999-2012, she served as the founding curator of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, New Mexico, where she curated or oversaw more than thirty exhibitions of works by O’Keeffe and her contemporaries.
Lynes was also the Founding Emily Fisher Landau Director of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center from 2001-2012.

Prior to her work at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, Lynes served as an independent consultant to the National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. from 1992-1999 and has taught art history at Vanderbilt University, Dartmouth College, Montgomery College, and the Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA).

Early works of Georgia O'Keeffe are works made before Georgia O'Keeffe was introduced to the principles of Arthur Wesley Dow in 1912, which were reflected in her watercolor paintings, O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914.
Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist.
She was best known for her paintings of enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes.
O'Keeffe has been recognized as the "Mother of American modernism".
Maria Chabot (1913–2001), was an advocate for Native American arts, a rancher, and a friend of Georgia O'Keeffe.
She was the general contractor for her house in Abiquiú, New Mexico and took the photograph of O'Keeffe entitled "Women Who Rode Away," in which the artist was on the back of a motorcycle.
Their correspondence was published in the book "Maria Chabot—Georgia O'Keeffe: Correspondence 1941-1949".

Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 represents Georgia O'Keeffe's first major exploration of abstract art and attainment of a freedom to explore her artistic talents based upon what she felt and envisioned.
O'Keeffe developed radical charcoal drawings, with just a few lines, that led to greater development of total abstraction.

This series of works was completed following three summers of instruction at the University of Virginia on Arthur Wesley Dow's design philosophies, which were highly influential in her development as an abstract artist.
Early the following year, photographer and art dealer, Alfred Stieglitz exhibited some of the drawings at his "291" art gallery.
A Storm, a 1922 pastel painting by Georgia O'Keeffe, shows lightning over a lake and the reflection of the moon.
The medium is pastel on paper, mounted on illustration board.
The painting is part of a collection of work depicting sea- and landscapes of Maine or Lake George, and were created by O'Keeffe between 1921 and 1922.
The technique and style of "A Storm" reflects O'Keeffe's earlier preference for charcoal as a medium.
It was donated to the Metropolitan Museum of Art by a private collector in 1981.
O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914 is an exhibition of watercolors that Georgia O'Keeffe created over three summers in the early 20th century at the University of Virginia.
Shown at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, the exhibit opened November 4, 2016 and runs through September 10, 2017.
Red and Orange Streak is a 1919 painting by Georgia O'Keeffe.
She painted it in New York, after arriving there from Texas.

In a letter to a friend, O'Keeffe wrote: "the whole thing—lit up—first in one place—then in another with flashes of lightning—sometimes just sheet lightning—and sometimes sheet lightning with a sharp bright zigzag flashing across—I … sat on the fence for a long time—just looking at the lightning."

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

hop1_docs: Early works of Georgia O'Keeffe | Early works of Georgia O'Keeffe are works made before Georgia O'Keeffe was introduced to the principles of Arthur Wesley Dow in 1912, which were reflected in her watercolor paintings, O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914.

Maria Chabot | Maria Chabot (1913–2001), was an advocate for Native American arts, a rancher, and a friend of Georgia O'Keeffe.  She was the general contractor for her house in Abiquiú, New Mexico and took the photograph of O'Keeffe entitled "Women Who Rode Away," in which the artist was on the back of a motorcycle.  Their correspondence was published in the book "Maria Chabot—Georgia O'Keeffe: Correspondence 1941-1949".

Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio | The Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio is a historic house museum at 12 Palvadera Drive in Abiquiú, New Mexico.  It was from 1945 until 1984 the principal residence and studio of artist Georgia O'Keeffe (1887-1986).  It is now owned and managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, through which guided tours can be arranged.  It was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998, as one of the most important artistic sites in the southwestern United States.

O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914 | O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914 is an exhibition of watercolors that Georgia O'Keeffe created over three summers in the early 20th century at the University of Virginia.  Shown at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, the exhibit opened November 4, 2016 and runs through September 10, 2017.

Georgia O'Keeffe Museum | The Georgia O'Keeffe Museum is dedicated to the artistic legacy of Georgia O'Keeffe, her life, American modernism, and public engagement.  It opened on July 17, 1997, eleven years after the artist's death, and is located at 217 Johnson Street in Santa Fe, New Mexico, United States.

Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist.  She was best known for her paintings of enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes.  O'Keeffe has been recognized as the "Mother of American modernism".

Barbara Buhler Lynes | Barbara Buhler Lynes is an art historian, curator, professor, and preeminent scholar on the art and life of Georgia O'Keeffe.  She is currently the Sunny Kaufman Senior Curator at the NSU Museum of Art in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.  From 1999-2012, she served as the founding curator of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, New Mexico, where she curated or oversaw more than thirty exhibitions of works by O’Keeffe and her contemporaries.  Lynes was also the Founding Emily Fisher Landau Director of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center from 2001-2012.  Prior to her work at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, Lynes served as an independent consultant to the National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. from 1992-1999 and has taught art history at Vanderbilt University, Dartmouth College, Montgomery College, and the Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA).

response.summary_2: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist. Georgia O'Keeffe Museum is dedicated to her artistic legacy and opened in Santa Fe, New Mexico, on July 17, 1997. Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio was her principal residence and studio from 1945 to 1984 and is now owned by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum.
response.answer: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist best known for paintings of enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes; the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe is dedicated to her artistic legacy.
